ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for onion uthappam; A0EJW (pancake) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for uthappam or South Indian fermented pancakes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium onion uthappam piece weighing approximately 120 g, as typically served in South Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Uttapam (Oothappam) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, ICMR-NIN
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports nutrient values for plain uttapam (oothappam) made from fermented rice and urad dal batter. Onion uthappam is the same base preparation with raw onion added as a topping before cooking. Nutrient values are adapted from the plain uttapam entry with a minor upward adjustment to dietary fibre and minor reduction in energy density to account for the onion topping (~10–15% by weight). Core macros are anchored to the ICMR-NIN plain uttapam reference.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of South Indian fermented foods: uttapam and related preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for onion uthappam was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 69 is estimated from the closest analogous entry for plain uttapam / fermented rice-lentil pancake reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by peer-reviewed literature on fermented South Indian foods (Shobana et al., 2007, European Journal of Clinical Nutrition). Onion topping is not expected to materially alter GI. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 37.8) / 100 ≈ 26.1, rounded to 26.0, using 120 g serving with 31.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~210 mg per 100 g) and portion size
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as sambar or pickle, as cumulative sodium from the full meal can exceed recommended limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 69) and moderate carbohydrate load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should consume onion uthappam in controlled portions (one piece) and pair it with high-fibre accompaniments such as sambar or vegetable chutney to attenuate the postprandial glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat content and cholesterol-free profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Onion uthappam is low in saturated fat and contains no cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly grain-based option when prepared with minimal oil and served without high-fat coconut chutneys.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion Uthappam (also spelled Onion Uttapam or Uttapa) is a thick, savoury South Indian pancake made from a fermented batter of rice and urad dal (black gram lentil). Unlike the thin and crispy dosa, uthappam is cooked as a soft, spongy disc approximately 1–1.5 cm thick, with diced or sliced raw onions pressed into the surface before the batter sets on a hot griddle or tawa. The fermentation process, typically 8–12 hours, develops a mild tangy flavour and improves the bioavailability of B vitamins and minerals. The onion topping adds a slight sweetness, crunch, and additional dietary fibre. Uthappam is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. It is a staple breakfast and tiffin item across Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, and Kerala, and is widely served in South Indian restaurants throughout Singapore and Malaysia. Nutritionally, onion uthappam provides moderate energy (~175 kcal per 100 g), a modest protein contribution from urad dal (~4.8 g per 100 g), and a medium glycemic index of approximately 69, placing it in the medium GI category. It is low in saturated fat and contains no cholesterol. Clinically, it is a reasonable carbohydrate source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when portion-controlled and paired with high-fibre accompaniments such as sambar. Sodium content is moderate and should be considered by individuals with hypertension when combined with salty condiments.
common_user_queries: How many calories in onion uttapam?
Is onion uthappam good for diabetics?
What is the difference between dosa and uthappam?
What is the GI of uttapam?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN plain uttapam entry, which is an approved primary source for Indian dishes. The onion variant is not separately listed in ICMR-NIN; values are adjusted to account for the onion topping. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for fermented rice-lentil pancakes, corroborated by peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain uttapam data; onion topping (~12% by weight) contributes minor additional fibre and vitamin C and slightly dilutes energy density. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for this plant-based, minimally processed preparation with no hydrogenated fats. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a vegan preparation containing no animal-derived ingredients. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no animal-derived ingredients are present and fermentation does not produce meaningful B12 in this preparation. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no animal-derived or fortified ingredients are used. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for plain uttapam and cannot be reliably estimated for the onion variant. 7. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. 8. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not separately reported in the ICMR-NIN source for uttapam; fermented lentil preparations are known to contain folate but a defensible numeric value is not available from the approved source. 9. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. 10. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. 11. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate; direct measurement for onion uthappam is not available in the University of Sydney GI Database. 12. The input record tagged this food under 'Malaysian' cuisine; however, uthappam is a South Indian dish of Tamil origin. The cuisine field has been corrected to 'Indian' with region_of_origin 'South India'. The food is served in Malaysian and Singaporean Indian restaurants. 13. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03302051 is used as the best available ontology anchor for uttapam; if this URI is not confirmed in the live FoodOn release, the match_type should be downgraded to closest_parent. 14. FoodEx2 code A0EJW is used as closest_parent for pancake; no specific code for uthappam exists in FoodEx2.

Added mandatory multilingual local_names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 for Singaporean-served Indian dishes.
Populated all per_100g nutrient fields using ICMR-NIN plain uttapam as primary source with documented adaptation for onion topping.
Added GI of 69 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database with full documentation of estimation basis.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes field to satisfy G3.
Documented all 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) with confirmed zero justification phrases in notes.
Ensured all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in flat aliases list to satisfy Q8 consistency rule.
Added specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions including Type 2 Diabetes with human_condition_name set correctly.
